report: The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a leftward head version and left arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. There is also a slight rightward head version with subtle right arm extension and rhythmic clonic jerks that spread bilaterally.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, there is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head version to the left. There is a subtle right arm flexion, which progresses to involve both arms in a tonic–clonic manner.

The patient initially sits upright with arms crossed, then leans back and lies down. There is a brief period of restlessness before she settles into a relaxed position, showing no signs of seizure activity.

The patient initially maintains a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version. They then exhibit right arm extension with the left arm remaining flexed, indicating fencer posturing, or display unilateral arm flexion, both of which transition into bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the left and right. There is a subtle right arm flexion, transitioning into a more pronounced leftward head version. The patient then shows bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and slight arm flexion. Gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity is observed, characterized by rhythmic jerking motions.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which then progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head turn to the left and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits an initial blank stare, followed by subtle head movements and asymmetric focal motor signs including unilateral head version (leftward or rightward) and mixed arm posturing—such as flexion of one arm with extension of the other, or isolated left arm flexion or right arm extension. These focal features progress gradually to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerks that spread to generalized convulsive movements.

The patient is lying still with a blank stare, followed by yawning and stretching. This progresses to a sudden leftward head version with left arm flexion and right arm extension, or bilateral head turning with arm flexion, transitioning into bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks that spread throughout the body. There is no visible movement or signs of seizure activity initially.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version and unilateral arm extension—either to the right or left—and progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version (to the left or right) accompanied by arm movement—either flexion or extension, most commonly right arm extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with rhythmic jerks observed during the clonic phase.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension in a fencer posture or rightward head version with right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive at the end.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension. In one instance, the initial movement involves a leftward head turn with right arm flexion, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with the patient turning onto the left side and then back to the right. In all other instances, the episode progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks following the rightward head version and right arm extension.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a head version to the right and extension of the right arm. This is succeeded by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness. In some instances, head turning occurs bilaterally with arm flexion before evolving into generalized convulsive activity. The seizure semiology consistently begins with oculomotor and unilateral upper extremity involvement, spreading to bilateral motor manifestations.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, there is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version to the left or right and corresponding unilateral arm movement—either flexion or extension. This is followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerking and loss of consciousness.

The patient initially sits with a blank stare, followed by a slight head version—either to the right or left—and variable involvement of the upper extremities. This includes subtle flexion or extension of the right or left arm, with right arm extension and rightward head version being more commonly observed. These focal features progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements in all instances, during which the patient becomes unresponsive. No significant tonic-clonic activity is present initially, and the patient remains relatively still at onset aside from the head and arm movements. The episode consistently evolves from a focal onset with motor signs to bilateral convulsive activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version and asymmetric motor signs including head turning to the right or left, with corresponding arm posturing—right arm extension (often with elbow flexion) or left arm extension, and occasional left arm flexion. These features progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version (predominantly to the right, with one instance to the left) and right arm extension. This is followed by initial arm and leg movements in some episodes. The seizure progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head turning—most commonly to the left, but also observed to the right—and associated asymmetric motor signs including arm flexion or extension. In some instances, the arms are raised, the head is tilted back, and the mouth is open before the head turns and motor activity begins. These initial features progress consistently to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerks, during which the patient becomes unresponsive.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity with rhythmic jerks, and the patient becomes unresponsive towards the end. Some episodes include initial head turning to the left and right with arm movements before evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic activity.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head turning—most notably to the right—and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic jerks and loss of consciousness.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and either right arm extension or right arm flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by either leftward or rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with rhythmic jerks observed in at least one instance.
Right arm extension with left arm flexion; head turns to the right. Bilateral tonic-clonic movements follow.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with the left arm remaining relaxed. This is followed by a gradual progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks. This includes right arm flexion with left arm extension and continued rhythmic clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version and arm movement—either rightward head version with right arm extension or leftward head version with left arm flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with rhythmic jerks observed throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle head version and arm flexion, either to the right or left, with subsequent progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In one instance, rightward head version and right arm flexion are followed by rhythmic clonic jerks spreading to both sides.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which subsequently spreads to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially lies with a blank stare and clasped hands, showing no visible movement or change in expression. This is followed by a slight rightward head version and right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. During the episode, the patient is observed holding a remote control.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by head version and arm movement—initially rightward head version with right arm extension, and at another time, leftward head version with left arm flexion. Both episodes progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version (leftward or rightward) and asymmetric arm posturing, including left arm flexion and/or right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks and associated head movement toward the side of the initial head version.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, with rhythmic clonic jerks involving both arms; during one episode, the patient's arms were held by staff. A sudden yawning and initial arm movements were also observed at the onset in one instance.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by left arm flexion and right arm extension in a fencer posture, with slight head version to the right. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially maintains a blank stare, followed by a subtle rightward head version and right arm movement—manifesting as either flexion or extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements involving both arms.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by subtle rightward head version and slight right arm flexion. Gradually, there is a progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and slight head movement, including a movement to the right. Subtle right arm flexion is observed, followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. There is no significant arm flexion or extension prior to the onset of clonic activity, and the patient remains relatively still throughout much of the video.
The patient maintains a blank stare while writing. There is no visible head version or arm flexion/extension. The patient remains seated upright throughout.
Blank stare, then rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic-clonic.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, there are bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version and hand movements. Subsequently, there is a transition to bilateral tonic-clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight head version—most commonly to the right, with one instance noted to the left. This is accompanied by noticeable right arm extension while the left arm remains relaxed. In some episodes, the right arm extension is subtle initially and progresses gradually. The head version may be brief and is sometimes associated with head down positioning or leaning forward, followed by a return to upright posture. Subsequently, the patient leans back and appears unresponsive. The episodes consistently progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking. These movements emerge from the initial focal features and involve the entire body.

The patient is seated, writing in a notebook. There are no observable signs of seizure activity such as blank staring, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains engaged in writing throughout the video.

The patient is seated at a table, writing in a notebook. There are no observable signs of seizure activity such as blank staring, lip smacking, head version, arm flexion or extension, tonic stiffening, clonic jerks, or spread to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The patient remains focused on writing throughout the video.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head versioning—specifically rightward—and associated arm movements, including right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic activity characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face and upper body.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially presents with a blank stare, followed by head version to the left and right and arm flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerks and accompanied by loss of consciousness and unresponsiveness.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by yawning, head version, and hand or arm movements. There is progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity, with the patient's arms moving in a rhythmic manner.

The patient initially presents with a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and extension of the right arm, along with raising both arms above the head and opening the mouth. This transitions into bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.
Arms raised above head with hands clasped, followed by a blank stare and transition to sudden bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by rhythmic arm motion.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. This is accompanied by arms raised, fists clenched, and a wide-open mouth, with head tilt observed during the episode. The seizure activity spreads to involve both arms in tonic–clonic fashion.
The patient begins with a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which evolves into bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks.

Initial arm flexion with hand clenching, arms raised and hands open, head slightly tilted back; blank stare followed by left arm flexion with right arm extension. Transition to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with open mouth and rhythmic jerks. Unresponsive at the end.

Arms raised with hands open and head slightly tilted back, followed by a blank stare, rightward head version, and right arm extension. Subsequently, there is progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, including rhythmic clonic activity.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient raises their right arm and opens their mouth wide, showing signs of agitation, accompanied by a blank stare and rightward head version with right arm extension. The movement progresses to involve both arms, with rhythmic jerking evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a rightward head version and subtle right arm movements, including extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking motions.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable motor signs including yawning and stretching, head version (rightward or bilateral), arm extension or flexion. In most instances, this progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks; however, one episode shows no significant movement or tonic-clonic activity.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by yawning and stretching in one instance. In other instances, the blank stare is followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which then progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. Head turning to the right and right arm extension is consistently observed prior to the onset of bilateral tonic-clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version and arm movement—either rightward head version with right arm extension or sudden leftward head version with left arm flexion. This is subsequently followed by progression to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, with rhythmic jerks observed during some episodes.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version and right arm extension, which may be subtle or brief. In some instances, there is left arm flexion or a slight head turn to the left, but the predominant pattern involves rightward head deviation and right arm involvement. This initial phase consistently progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, often characterized by rhythmic jerks. The seizure activity typically spreads from unilateral onset to involve both sides in a tonic-clonic manner.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which may be slight or subtle in some instances. In certain observations, there is brief unilateral arm flexion on the left side or subtle arm flexion and head movements instead of clear extension. These initial features consistently progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In most cases, the bilateral tonic-clonic phase includes rhythmic jerking movements, observed throughout the episode. The spread of activity often begins focally with right-sided features before generalizing to involve both sides of the body.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a head version—most commonly to the right, but occasionally to the left—accompanied by unilateral motor signs. These include subtle or brief extension or flexion of the right or left arm, with occasional left-sided arm flexion noted prior to generalization. The episode consistently progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic jerking and involvement of all extremities. In most instances, the initial lateralizing signs involve a rightward head turn and right arm involvement, though leftward head version with left arm flexion is also observed in some reports. Generalization to bilateral tonic-clonic activity occurs across all observations.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, with or without lip smacking, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. This is subsequently followed by bilateral tonic-clonic movements, which may include rhythmic jerks.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a sudden rightward head version and right arm extension. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, which in some instances include rhythmic jerks. In one observation, the patient exhibited yawning and opening their mouth wide after the blank stare, without any head version, arm flexion, or tonic-clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by yawning and head version to the left and right, with right arm extension and left arm flexion. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by lip smacking or yawning. This is followed by head version, typically to the right but occasionally to the left or bilaterally, and asymmetric arm movements including extension (commonly right arm) and/or flexion (sometimes contralateral). In some instances, there is rightward head version with right arm extension, or leftward head version with mixed right arm extension and left arm flexion. These focal features progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements, characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks and unresponsiveness. The evolution from initial staring to asymmetric motor signs and subsequent generalization to bilateral tonic-clonic activity is consistent across observations.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which subsequently spreads to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. In some instances, the head version and arm extension are described as slight or subtle, with gradual progression to more generalized convulsive activity involving both sides of the body.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version or head turn to the right, occasionally with leftward deviation or head tilt. There is a subtle or noticeable right arm extension in most episodes, with occasional right arm flexion or mixed right arm flexion and left arm extension; the left arm typically remains relaxed initially. These focal features consistently progress to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks that spread to both sides, involving both arms and associated with loss of consciousness. The sequence begins with a blank stare and lateralizing signs to the right side, evolving into generalized motor activity.

The patient consistently exhibits an initial blank stare, often accompanied by a rightward head version and subtle right arm flexion or extension. In several instances, this is followed by lip smacking or slight head movement to the right. Right arm involvement is prominent early in the sequence, with some episodes showing right arm flexion and contralateral left arm extension resembling fencer posturing. The seizures progress from focal onset to bilateral motor involvement, characterized by rhythmic clonic jerks or evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic movements. One episode includes a head version to the left prior to generalization. All events ultimately involve bilateral motor activity following the initial lateralized features.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by head version (leftward or rightward) and asymmetric arm movements, including left arm flexion, right arm extension, or bilateral involvement. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking and loss of consciousness. In one instance, the patient regains focus post-ictally, sits up, and holds hands together.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version. There is subtle right arm involvement—either flexion or extension—that progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements or rhythmic clonic jerks involving both sides. In most instances, the seizure evolves from focal onset with motor signs on the right side to generalized bilateral tonic–clonic activity.

The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by a slight rightward head version and right arm extension, with the left arm remaining relaxed in some observations. This is accompanied by subtle head movements and slight arm flexion in early phases, progressing to rhythmic bilateral jerks and subsequently to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. The seizure evolution consistently shows a pattern beginning with a blank stare and unilateral features (rightward head version and right arm extension), which then generalizes to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.

The patient initially lies still, then raises both arms with hands open; there are no obvious signs of tonic–clonic movements or head version at this stage. Subsequently, the patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, which may be subtle in some instances. This progresses to bilateral tonic-clonic movements.
The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerks and subsequent unresponsiveness.
